name: Harry James
abstract: Harry Haag James (March 15, 1916 – July 5, 1983) was an American musician who is best known as a trumpet-playing band leader who led a big band to great commercial success from 1939 to 1946. He broke up his band for a short period in 1947, but shortly after he reorganized and was active again with his band from then until his death in 19…

about_quotes.speaker: Paul Smith
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee mentions Harry James only in response to the interviewer's question about band leaders who 'called it quits' in 1946, stating 'No I don’t think they called it quits'. There is no indication of a personal or professional relationship between Paul Smith and Harry James.
about_quotes.doc_title: Paul Smith interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, February 14, 1999

about_quotes.speaker: Tom Varner
about_quotes.evidence: Glen Ferris, who is a fantastic trombone player that had played with both Frank Zappa and Harry James... And at one point Harry James told the lead trombone player or the trumpet player, it’s like, “That kid is pretty good but tell him to stop conducting the band.”
about_quotes.doc_title: Tom Varner interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, August 13, 2025

about_quotes.speaker: Konitz Lee
about_quotes.evidence: Probably you know, Glen Miller, and Harry James and some of those bands that were recording for the Chesterfield cigarettes’ fifteen-minute program. My mother and I used to listen to them.
about_quotes.doc_title: Konitz Lee — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Milt Fillius
about_quotes.evidence: So we went out and talked to him and said, “Geez I sure would like to see that band. He said, “Well,” he says, “I’ll tell you what, meet me here at intermission and I’ll take you in.”
about_quotes.doc_title: Milt Fillius interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, May 18, 2000

about_quotes.speaker: Burrill Crohn
about_quotes.evidence: I first became aware of the sound of the trumpet... It was Harry James... my early taste was to swing, because the people I listened to were the big bands and Harry James.
about_quotes.doc_title: Burrill Crohn interviewed by Monk Rowe, Woodstock, New York, January 3, 2012

about_quotes.speaker: Glenn Zottola
about_quotes.evidence: He says, 'I’ve had everybody in my bands,' and he starts listing all these trumpet players: Harry James, Cootie Williams, on and on.
about_quotes.doc_title: Glenn Zottola interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, December 7, 2020
